sentences.text: I know the-- where the grave is.
In-- after--after in Krakow, a couple miles from my hometown.
Przemysl is a different city-- Galicia.
See, I know-- because, you see, I was in Slomniki.
From Slomniki I go to a farmer who lived over there.
Their neighbor told me, come on.
I'll show you where your father and mother is.
And even I go to the camp, it was the Nazis officer over there.
I told her, I can't go with you.
You are-- you are a officer, German.
And after Slomniki, a couple miles from Slomniki, they show me.
That's a grave with a lot-- a lot of Jewish people.
That's your mother over here, and your father's over here, together.
Slomniki, oh, about four hours ago by horses.
Today, I don't know how long it would take.
[LAUGHS] And after this, go-- but first, I come, I was go to Milan.
In Milan, they told me, come on over there.
And I would go-- I travel a little, a little travel in Italy.
In Rome, I was in Rome.
I saw-- I saw the Pope, [NON-ENGLISH].
I told them I don't want your money.
I can do nothing for you.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You see, the Pope that is now is Polish.
My mother tell me, you know this guy?
He got the book, the Bible.
My mother asked you, what you do with them?
He wants to be a priest.
And my mother told him, you be big if you're a priest.
My father told me to do something for him, for the fathers.
For this time, I know him.
Oh, you be a big boy, a big man.
[LAUGHS] Everybody told me, why you not go to him?
I told them, he's an old man.
And go from here to Italy again?
I be staying in Italy, I was-- I make a petition to stay in Italy.
The government told me, you cannot stay.
They say, you know the poor people here after the war.
I wish I could talk to him.
And I can't talk to him.
I tell them I talk to him.
Oh, but then the Polish Pope was over there, difference.
But he come to stand where I was.
Because you see, they told me-- I never got work for my people, the Italian people.
I came from Napoli over here.
They say, the ship it not go yet.
And up, that way we going.
First I got to New York.
And from New York, I get my transport over here because I come over here like a tailor.
You know he give me my raise?
But Hickey Freeman, you see, but Hickey Freeman is Italian people.
Most of them were Italian before.
They're asking me, do you speak Italian?
The woman's said, hey, you stop with the holler at this guy.
And after, I go someplace else.
He no like if somebody the Italian people you like it.
After this I was going Schuler Potato Chips.
And after this I go someplace called Gerber's baby food.
Putting it in the jars by the machine.
Today over here you've got to work, not for nothing money.
My son was about three years.
I worked with them about 18 years, 19 years.
Because young people, they want to work.
They come pretty soon-- they come over here about Friday, Saturday, I got over here.
I can tell you once the leaves-- a-- a lesson, a lesson.
Tell them, the people again-- make ready.
Nobody or nothing, just be ready for every minute.
You never know what's going to happen over here.
I tell you what it is.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No different today-- Jews, the Christians-- because the situation is very bad now.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The people now-- oh, no, nothing could be-- All right.
For me, I wish them good luck.
For me, I not go to the war because I watch.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I wish-- I wish I could do something more for the people.
And I can't do nothing more.
I'm an older man, an old man.
Well, I do how much I can.
And I said, you see, when you have life to live, no different how you survive.
I was in the Nazi camp, five years.
And come over here to Italy, from Italy over here.
And I started working, catch a couple dollars.
And like that, come up a little.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then I got friends that come over here.
And I tell you, people, they look at Japs.
The government have to help them.
I-- I not tell the government, help me.
I worry for the young people, the young generation.
You know, the situation is the war, another war.
And I don't want the people-- get be killing in a situation like this.
Tell you the truth. the people-- the American people do not care for that.
We people from Europe, we knows.
We know the wars with everything.
And I told them, stay ready.
If you need to go to fight, you have to fight-- nothing you can do.
Make it a good army, and make it the guns ready in every minute.
If you go wait till the war come up, it will be too late.
Same like me-- the war coming up, the German coming up.
And right away they took me to the army, and then go with them.
You have to go like that.
You have to save the country.
It's either this or there will be another one.
I have got all the friends over there.
The shul is old, about 80 years.
You see, this house and the synagogue, it's the same age.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a German section.
Over here was a German that lived over here.
They are the one selling me this house.
So I have no one to sell him his house.
And it has been until May.
It's up to people-- how the people go.
And the police get me on, they hang up.
What kind of story for instance?
I go to say again the same to the young people-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to take care of yourself and watch your steps.
I don't know, probably in Galicia they took it.
And this is the rest of them, all of my friends.
No, wait a minute-- in '51, I come over here.
This photograph, they make this in Italy.
There was I think so 19-- 1948 or 1947.
I think it's in Israel they make the picture.
Oh, he was about 60 years old.
Who knows what day they make that.
Well, I was after the war.
After the war I was in Italy for work.
I know that we worked together.
I think that's supposed to be me in the picture.
Oh, I think they was married a couple years ago.
